chunk.title: Supported Cloud Providers
chunk.text: CloudDash supports infrastructure monitoring integrations for AWS, Google Cloud Platform, and Microsoft Azure. Supported AWS services include CloudWatch metrics, CloudTrail audit events, EC2, RDS, EKS, Lambda, and billing exports. Supported GCP services include Cloud Monitoring metrics, Cloud Logging, Compute Engine, GKE, Cloud SQL, and billing exports. Supported Azure services include Azure Monitor metrics, Activity Log, Virtual Machines, AKS, Azure SQL, and cost management exports. CloudDash does not currently provide a documented Datadog integration in the customer support knowledge base. If a customer asks about an unsupported provider or third-party monitoring platform, acknowledge that the knowledge base does not confirm support and offer escalation to the product team or a feature request.

chunk.title: Resetting a CloudDash API Key
chunk.text: Workspace admins can reset a CloudDash API key from Settings > Developer > API Keys. Choose the affected key, select Rotate key, copy the new secret, and update any services that call the CloudDash API. The old secret stops working immediately after rotation. If an API key may have been exposed publicly, rotate it immediately and review audit logs for unusual API usage. CloudDash support cannot view or recover full API key secrets.

chunk.title: Inviting Team Members
chunk.text: Workspace admins can invite team members from Settings > Members > Invite member. Enter the user's email address, choose a role, and select Send invite. Invitations expire after 7 days. If an invitation expires, resend it from the Members page. Enterprise workspaces using SSO may require the user to be assigned to the CloudDash application in the identity provider before the invitation can be accepted.

chunk.title: Configuring Alert Thresholds
chunk.text: Alert thresholds are configured from Alerts > Policies. Select an existing policy or create a new one, choose the monitored resource, metric, comparison operator, threshold value, evaluation window, and notification channel. For noisy alerts, increase the evaluation window or add a minimum data point requirement. For alerts that fire too late, reduce the evaluation window and verify that the metric is arriving at the expected interval. Pro and Enterprise workspaces can define multi-condition alert policies.

chunk.title: Alerts Not Firing After Updating AWS Credentials
chunk.text: If CloudDash alerts stop firing after AWS integration credentials are updated, first verify that the new IAM role or access key has permission to read CloudWatch metrics and list tagged resources. Required permissions include cloudwatch:GetMetricData, cloudwatch:ListMetrics, tag:GetResources, and sts:GetCallerIdentity. Next, open Integrations > AWS > Connection test and confirm the account status is Healthy. If the connection test fails, re-enter the role ARN or access key and secret. If the connection is healthy but alerts still do not fire, open Alerts > Policies and confirm the policies are enabled, notification channels are active, and the monitored resources still match the policy filters. Finally, wait one full evaluation window after credential repair before testing again because CloudDash evaluates alerts after fresh

chunk.title: Alerts Not Firing After Updating AWS Credentials
chunk.text: monitored resources still match the policy filters. Finally, wait one full evaluation window after credential repair before testing again because CloudDash evaluates alerts after fresh metric ingestion.

chunk.title: AWS CloudWatch Integration Failing
chunk.text: When AWS CloudWatch integration setup fails, confirm the AWS account ID, region list, and IAM role ARN are correct. The IAM trust policy must allow CloudDash's external ID shown in the integration setup screen. The role must allow CloudWatch metric reads, tag reads, and STS identity verification. If the integration previously worked, check whether the IAM role was renamed, the external ID was changed, or a service control policy started blocking CloudWatch APIs. After updating permissions, run the CloudDash connection test and refresh the integration.

chunk.title: Dashboard Loading Slowly
chunk.text: Large dashboards can load slowly when they contain many high-cardinality widgets, long time ranges, or cross-cloud queries. Reduce the time range, remove unused widgets, and split unrelated infrastructure views into separate dashboards. If only one widget is slow, edit the widget and check whether it queries a high-cardinality label such as instance ID, pod name, or request path. Enterprise customers can contact support to review query performance for shared executive dashboards.

chunk.title: CloudDash API Authentication
chunk.text: CloudDash API requests use bearer token authentication. Send the API key in the Authorization header as Bearer <token>. Do not send API keys in query strings. Example curl request: curl -H "Authorization: Bearer $CLOUDDASH_API_KEY" https://api.clouddash.example/v1/monitors. API keys are workspace-scoped and inherit the permissions selected when the key is created. Rotate keys regularly and immediately after suspected exposure.

chunk.title: API Rate Limits
chunk.text: CloudDash API rate limits are enforced per workspace. Starter workspaces allow 60 requests per minute, Pro workspaces allow 600 requests per minute, and Enterprise workspaces allow custom limits agreed in the contract. When a client exceeds its limit, the API returns HTTP 429 with Retry-After and X-RateLimit-Reset headers. Clients should use exponential backoff and avoid retry storms. If a Pro customer consistently needs higher limits, recommend Enterprise plan review.

chunk.title: Webhook Configuration
chunk.text: Webhooks can be configured from Settings > Developer > Webhooks. Add the destination URL, choose event types, and copy the signing secret. CloudDash signs webhook requests with the X-CloudDash-Signature header. The receiver should verify the signature and return a 2xx response within 10 seconds. Failed deliveries are retried for up to 24 hours. Alert event webhooks are available on Pro and Enterprise plans.

chunk.title: Setting Up SSO
chunk.text: SSO is available for Enterprise workspaces. To configure SSO, open Settings > Security > SSO, choose SAML 2.0, and copy the CloudDash ACS URL and Entity ID into the identity provider. Then paste the identity provider metadata URL or XML into CloudDash and run Test connection. If users cannot sign in, confirm the NameID format is email address, the user's email matches their CloudDash member email, and the user is assigned to the CloudDash app in the identity provider. SSO enforcement should only be enabled after a successful test login by an admin.

chunk.title: RBAC Roles and Permissions
chunk.text: CloudDash includes Admin, Editor, Viewer, and Billing Admin roles. Admins can manage integrations, alerts, users, and security settings. Editors can create and edit dashboards, monitors, and alert policies. Viewers can read dashboards and alert history but cannot change configuration. Billing Admins can view invoices and manage plan changes but cannot change monitoring configuration. Enterprise workspaces can request custom roles through support.

chunk.title: Using Audit Logs
chunk.text: Audit logs show important workspace events such as user invitations, role changes, API key rotation, integration updates, SSO changes, and billing admin actions. Admins can view audit logs from Settings > Security > Audit Logs. Pro workspaces retain audit logs for 90 days. Enterprise workspaces retain audit logs for 365 days and can export them through the API.

chunk.title: Starter, Pro, and Enterprise Plan Comparison
chunk.text: CloudDash offers Starter, Pro, and Enterprise plans. Starter includes basic dashboards, standard cloud integrations, and limited API access. Pro includes advanced alerting, alert webhooks, longer audit logs, higher API limits, and multi-condition alert policies. Enterprise includes SSO, custom API limits, custom roles, longer audit retention, priority support, and contract-based terms. Exact pricing is not included in the support knowledge base. If a customer asks for a custom quote or contract terms, route the request to Billing or Sales support.

chunk.title: Upgrade and Downgrade Rules
chunk.text: Plan upgrades take effect immediately after confirmation by a Billing Admin. If a customer upgrades from Pro to Enterprise, Enterprise-only features such as SSO and custom roles become available after the workspace plan refreshes. Downgrades are scheduled for the end of the current billing period to avoid removing features unexpectedly. Only Billing Admins can request plan changes. If a plan change is connected to a technical blocker, resolve or document the technical issue before completing the billing handover.

chunk.title: Understanding CloudDash Invoices
chunk.text: CloudDash invoices include the subscription plan, billing period, workspace identifier, taxes when applicable, and any prorated adjustments from plan changes. Usage-based line items may appear for contracted Enterprise customers. Billing Admins can download invoices from Settings > Billing > Invoices. If a customer disputes a line item, collect the invoice number, billing period, workspace ID, and the specific line item before deciding whether to resolve through policy guidance or escalate.

chunk.title: Duplicate Charge and Refund Escalation Policy
chunk.text: If a customer reports a duplicate charge, ask for the invoice number, billing period, last four digits of the payment method if available, and workspace ID. Billing support can explain invoice details and collect dispute information, but immediate refunds and manager requests must be escalated to a human billing specialist. Mark duplicate charge cases as high priority when the customer says they were charged twice, requests an immediate refund, or asks to speak to a manager. Do not promise that a refund has been approved unless a human billing specialist confirms it.

chunk.title: Payment Failure Troubleshooting
chunk.text: When a payment fails, CloudDash notifies Billing Admins by email and displays a banner in Settings > Billing. Customers should confirm that the card has not expired, the billing address matches the card issuer records, and the bank has not blocked the transaction. Billing Admins can update payment details from Settings > Billing > Payment method. CloudDash retries failed payments automatically for 7 days before account features may be limited.
